keyFindings.text: **KNOWN, UNCHANGED (C1b, gap 4)** — `loadExtension()` still throws `"not implemented"` in both API flavors [SOURCE: bindings/javascript/packages/common/compat.ts:188-190; promise.ts:450-452]; the entire C-API loadable-extension surface is ❌ [SOURCE: COMPAT.md:756-764]; SQL `load_extension(X)` is partial — "Only Turso-native extensions, not SQLite .so/.dll", two-arg form ❌ [SOURCE: COMPAT.md:287-288]. **sqlite-vec still cannot load into Turso.** Native libSQL-compatible vector functions remain the substitute [SOURCE: COMPAT.md:1005-1018]. The baseline claim that the libsql npm package DOES support `loadExtension()` could not be re-verified (web blocked) — carried forward as known-unverified.

**KNOWN, UNCHANGED (C1c, gap 5)** — JS UDF registration still absent: `function()`, `aggregate()`, and `table()` all throw `"not implemented"` in both APIs [SOURCE: compat.ts:176-186; promise.ts:438-448]; C API `sqlite3_create_function*` and the `sqlite3_result_*` family are ❌/stubs [SOURCE: COMPAT.md:635-666]. Baseline workaround W5a (keep logic in TypeScript) stands.

**KNOWN, UNCHANGED + NEW mitigation (C1d, gap 11)** — `backup()` and `serialize()` still throw in both APIs [SOURCE: compat.ts:168-174; promise.ts:430-436]; C API backup and serialize/deserialize remain ❌ stubs [SOURCE: COMPAT.md:680-771]. NEW vs baseline: `VACUUM INTO` is supported (plain VACUUM experimental) [SOURCE: COMPAT.md:130] and works on attached databases [SOURCE: CHANGELOG.md:237] — a transactional online-backup substitute beyond baseline's file-copy workaround; Turso-specific WAL primitives (`libsql_wal_frame_count`/`get_frame`/`insert_frame`/`disable_checkpoint`) are ✅ [SOURCE: COMPAT.md:787-794].

**NEW (C1a follow-on)** — Two pragmas our init sequence sets remain unsupported at engine level even though the `.pragma()` method now exists: `synchronous` supports only `OFF`/`FULL` (we set `NORMAL`) [SOURCE: COMPAT.md:201], and `mmap_size` is ❌ (we set 268435456) [SOURCE: COMPAT.md:183]. `busy_timeout`, `cache_size`, `foreign_keys`, `temp_store` are all ✅ [SOURCE: COMPAT.md:147,148,165,205]. `PRAGMA foreign_key_list` was newly implemented in 0.6.0 [SOURCE: CHANGELOG.md:28].

**NEW (C1a, gap 3)** — Engine-level verdicts for the six asked PRAGMAs: `journal_mode` ✅ [SOURCE: COMPAT.md:177], `wal_checkpoint` 🚧 partial (parameterized form like `wal_checkpoint(TRUNCATE)` unsupported) [SOURCE: COMPAT.md:215], `user_version` ✅ [SOURCE: COMPAT.md:209], `quick_check` ✅ [SOURCE: COMPAT.md:191], `database_list` ✅ [SOURCE: COMPAT.md:158], `table_info` ✅ [SOURCE: COMPAT.md:202].

**NEW (C1e)** — Fate of sync `.transaction()` wrappers answered: compat ships a full better-sqlite3-style `transaction(fn)` with `.deferred`/`.immediate`/`.exclusive` variants implemented as `BEGIN <mode>`/`COMMIT`/`ROLLBACK` [SOURCE: compat.ts:116-146]. Caveat: plain BEGIN only — no SAVEPOINT fallback, so better-sqlite3's nested-transaction semantics are not replicated. `interrupt()` throws in both APIs [SOURCE: compat.ts:230-232; promise.ts:494-496]. The async API moved to `prepare()`-returns-promise in 0.6.0 [SOURCE: CHANGELOG.md:152] and gained per-connection/per-statement query timeouts [SOURCE: CHANGELOG.md:79; compat.ts:28-46].

**NEW (C1f)** — ATTACH DATABASE ✅ and DETACH DATABASE ✅ in the statement matrix [SOURCE: COMPAT.md:88,99], with active 0.5.0→0.6.0 hardening: ATTACH initializes fresh databases [SOURCE: CHANGELOG.md:71], optimizer index-resolution fix for attach [SOURCE: CHANGELOG.md:361], canonical schema stored for attached DBs [SOURCE: CHANGELOG.md:55], encryption keys for attached DBs via URI params [SOURCE: CHANGELOG.md:514]. The vector-shard architecture is plausible at 0.7.0-pre.6, BUT one contradiction needs runtime validation: the expression matrix still lists `schema.table.column` as 🚧 "Schemas aren't supported" [SOURCE: COMPAT.md:241], and `sqlite_dbpage` has "no attach support" [SOURCE: COMPAT.md:1117]. Qualified `alias.table.column` references against attached DBs must be smoke-tested before committing.

**NEW, REFUTED (C1a, gap 3)** — Baseline claimed "`.pragma()` method not available — neither Turso's SDK nor the libsql npm package implements it." Turso's own JS SDK at 0.7.0-pre.6 implements `pragma()` in BOTH the sync compat API and the async promise API; it prepares `PRAGMA ${source}` and returns `stmt.all()` [SOURCE: bindings/javascript/packages/common/compat.ts:148-166; bindings/javascript/packages/common/promise.ts:410-428]. Caveat: better-sqlite3's `{ simple: true }` option is accepted but silently ignored — it always returns an array of row objects.

**PARTIAL, CHANGED-strengthened (C1e, gap 16)** — The better-sqlite3-compatible sync mode is shipped, not speculative: `@tursodatabase/database@0.7.0-pre.6` exports `"."` → async `promise.js` and `"./compat"` → sync `compat.js` [SOURCE: bindings/javascript/packages/native/package.json:2-15]. Sync mode drives the engine via `connectSync()` plus a `stepSync()`/`ioLoopSync()` pump on the JS thread — confirming the baseline caveat that it's a synchronously-pumped I/O loop, not native sync [SOURCE: compat.ts:78-89, 202-225, 317-343].

**KNOWN | UNCHANGED (C2a, gap 1)** — No native vector index at v0.7.0-pre.6; similarity search is still a full linear scan. The vendored docs state it verbatim: "Vector indexes are not yet supported. All similarity searches use a linear scan over the table." [SOURCE: .opencode/specs/z_future/sqlite-to-turso/external/turso-main/docs/sql-reference/functions/vector.mdx:392-394]. COMPAT.md's vector section lists only scalar creation/distance/utility functions — no index entries [SOURCE: external/turso-main/COMPAT.md:1005-1018]. `rg` for `diskann|vector_top_k|libsql_vector_idx` across `core/` and `cli/` returns zero engine hits.

**KNOWN | UNCHANGED (C2b)** — Distance semantics are drop-in score-compatible: `vector_distance_cos` is defined as `1 - cosine_similarity` [SOURCE: external/turso-main/docs/sql-reference/functions/vector.mdx:134-136], the same definition sqlite-vec's `vec_distance_cosine` uses, so result ordering and any stored score thresholds are preserved.

**KNOWN | UNCHANGED (C2c)** — The 003/002 §5 scaling table remains the operative planning model: 2k <10ms; 5k ~15-25ms; 10k ~50-100ms (marginal); 50k ~300-500ms (no); 100k 700ms+ (no) [SOURCE: research/003 - gaps-and-workarounds-sqlite-to-turso.md:100-106; research/002 - recommendations-sqlite-to-turso-migration.md:118]. Nothing in the vendored tree changes the O(n) premise (linear scan confirmed above). For a ~10k corpus the verdict is still "marginal". Distance ops have dedicated Rust implementations (core/vector/operations/distance_cos.rs etc.), so absolute milliseconds may beat the estimates, but no benchmark was run — treat the table's shape as high-confidence, its absolute numbers as medium-confidence.

**NEW (C2a)** — Index groundwork exists but is experimental/disabled. The core tree has a pluggable index-method extension point containing a *toy* sparse-IVF vector index [SOURCE: external/turso-main/core/index_method/mod.rs, core/index_method/toy_vector_sparse_ivf.rs, tests/integration/index_method/mod.rs]. Separately, the JS conformance suite contains a `test.skip`'d test exercising the full libSQL ANN surface — `FLOAT32(8)` column, `CREATE INDEX ... libsql_vector_idx(embedding)`, and `vector_top_k('t_idx', ...)` [SOURCE: external/turso-main/testing/conformance/javascript/__test__/sync.test.js:277-300]. Turso is converging on the libSQL-compatible ANN API (the API baseline W1c relies on), but it is not callable in the local engine yet.

**NEW (C2b)** — Binary blob format is migration-compatible with sqlite-vec for float32. Turso's dense f32 vector blob is a raw little-endian f32 array with length exactly `dims * 4` — no header, no trailing type byte (only the Float8/Float1Bit formats carry trailing metadata bytes 0x04/0x03) [SOURCE: external/turso-main/core/vector/vector_types.rs:54,67,213,346-347, `from_f32` at :90]. sqlite-vec stores float32 vectors in the same raw f32 LE layout, so existing `vec_memories` embedding blobs (and `to_embedding_buffer` output) can be bulk-copied without re-encoding.

**NEW (C2b)** — Concrete `vec_memories` migration path, refined vs baseline W1a: (1) replace `CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE vec_memories USING vec0(... FLOAT[384] ...)` (vector-index-schema.ts:1176-1179 per 003:31-36) with a plain table `(rowid INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, embedding BLOB)` — the repo's per-dimension fallback blob tables (`vector_table_name_for_profile`) already model this [SOURCE: context/context-report.md:101]; (2) bulk-copy blobs as-is (format-compatible, above); (3) rewrite KNN queries from vec0 `MATCH`/`k=` and `vec_distance_cosine` calls (vector-index-queries.ts:403,407 per context-report.md:113) to `SELECT rowid, vector_distance_cos(embedding, ?) AS d FROM ... ORDER BY d ASC LIMIT k`; (4) dimension enforcement moves from DDL (`FLOAT[384]`) to runtime — distance functions error on dim mismatch [SOURCE: vector.mdx:145] — so keep the existing per-dimension table routing; (5) the `writeVectorsToKnn` transaction-wrapped delete+insert blocker (reindex.ts:370 per context-report.md:34) dissolves into ordinary DML once vec0 is gone. Caveat: the ATTACH/`VACUUM INTO` shard blockers (context-report.md:41,46-47) are separate gaps and unchanged by the vector layer.

**NEW (C2c)** — A mitigation lever absent from baseline workarounds W1a-W1d: quantized vectors (`vector8`, `vector1bit` + `vector_distance_jaccard`) [SOURCE: vector.mdx:88-126,215-228] enable a cheap coarse-scan → exact-rerank two-stage pattern that could push brute-force acceptability past 10k without waiting for ANN.

**PARTIAL | CHANGED (C2b)** — The native function surface is broader than the baseline recorded. Baseline 003 listed only `vector_distance_cos` and `vector_distance_l2` [SOURCE: research/003 - gaps-and-workarounds-sqlite-to-turso.md:42-43]. v0.7.0-pre.6 additionally ships `vector_distance_dot` (negative dot product), `vector_distance_jaccard` (binary vectors), quantized creation functions `vector8` and `vector1bit`, plus `vector_extract`, `vector_concat`, `vector_slice` [SOURCE: external/turso-main/docs/sql-reference/functions/vector.mdx:88-228; COMPAT.md:1011-1018].

**KNOWN / UNCHANGED (C3a, gap 2)** — FTS at 0.7.0-pre.6 is still Tantivy-based, not FTS5: "Turso implements FTS using Tantivy instead of SQLite's FTS3/FTS4/FTS5" with FTS3/FTS4/FTS5 explicitly ❌ [SOURCE: external/turso-main/COMPAT.md:1073-1084]. The baseline's "no FTS5, full rewrite required" framing holds.

**KNOWN / UNCHANGED (C3a, gap 2)** — The query surface assumed by workarounds W2a/W2b is intact and first-class: `fts_match`, `fts_score`, `fts_highlight` all ✅ in the compat matrix [SOURCE: external/turso-main/COMPAT.md:1079-1082], registered as native functions in core [SOURCE: external/turso-main/core/function.rs:419-421, 1763-1765], implemented in the index module [SOURCE: external/turso-main/core/index_method/fts.rs:81,160], and documented as the official three-function API [SOURCE: external/turso-main/docs/sql-reference/functions/fts.mdx:13].

**KNOWN / UNCHANGED (C3b, gap 13)** — NoMergePolicy is still hard-wired: the writer sets it unconditionally (`writer.set_merge_policy(Box::new(NoMergePolicy))`) [SOURCE: external/turso-main/core/index_method/fts.rs:2956, import at :31], and the docs list "No automatic segment merging — uses NoMergePolicy, use OPTIMIZE INDEX for manual segment merging" as a current limitation [SOURCE: external/turso-main/docs/fts.md:455]. Baseline workaround W13a (scheduled `OPTIMIZE INDEX`) remains required.

**KNOWN / UNCHANGED (context)** — The live-code blocker surface is unchanged in character: the migration must replace the FTS5 gate (`bm25-index.ts:478`, `:520`), the `memory_fts` virtual table + 3 sync triggers (`vector-index-schema.ts:1194-1223`), FTS5 `delete-all`/`rebuild` commands (`checkpoints.ts:1606`, `:1930`), and the retention sweep's FTS5 optimize [SOURCE: context/context-report.md:35-50,111-124]. The baseline's claimed upside — Tantivy is transactionally integrated, eliminating the three sync triggers (003:159, 454-455) — is consistent with the vendored design docs (tombstones applied at merge, atomic commit) [SOURCE: external/turso-main/docs/fts.md:26,56-57].

**NEW (C3a)** — FTS is gated behind an explicit experimental feature named `index_method`: "CREATE INDEX ... USING for FTS and custom index types" is listed in the experimental-features table [SOURCE: external/turso-main/docs/sql-reference/experimental-features.mdx:19], enabled via CLI flag `--experimental-index-method` [SOURCE: experimental-features.mdx:41], Rust builder `experimental_index_method(true)` [SOURCE: experimental-features.mdx:58], and a JS SDK `experimental: [...]` connection-option array [SOURCE: experimental-features.mdx:80-86]; create-index docs confirm "This feature is experimental and must be enabled before use" [SOURCE: external/turso-main/docs/sql-reference/statements/create-index.mdx:101]. The baseline gap-2 workaround (003:141-199) never mentions this enablement prerequisite — W2a's `CREATE INDEX ... USING fts` will fail without it. Migration plans must add the experimental flag to every connection that touches the FTS index.

**NEW (C3a)** — FTS shipped into the JS SDK path since the baseline: CHANGELOG entries "Enable FTS for JavaScript SDK" [SOURCE: external/turso-main/CHANGELOG.md:681], "Enable fts in sdk-kit" [SOURCE: CHANGELOG.md:241], "deps: upgrade tantivy and make default feature in sdk-kit" [SOURCE: CHANGELOG.md:170], and "Remove fts/tantivy as default dependency in turso-core" [SOURCE: CHANGELOG.md:810] — i.e., FTS is now an SDK-level default feature while remaining opt-in (experimental) at the SQL surface. A JS-binding test exercises FTS [SOURCE: external/turso-main/bindings/javascript/packages/native/promise.test.ts].

**NEW (C3b)** — Small ergonomic improvement: a bare `OPTIMIZE INDEX;` now optimizes ALL FTS indexes in the database, alongside the per-index form [SOURCE: external/turso-main/docs/fts.md:421-430]; the optimize path flushes pending docs, merges all segments into one, and reloads the reader [SOURCE: external/turso-main/core/index_method/fts.rs:3301-3382]. W13a's maintenance task can use the no-name form instead of enumerating indexes.

**PARTIAL / UNCHANGED-by-default (C3c, gap 15)** — The libsql FTS5 parameterized-insert bug could NOT be verified upstream this iteration: the vendored tree is turso-core, not the libsql repo, so it carries no evidence about issue #1811; WebFetch/WebSearch permissions were denied for this seat. Baseline claim stands as written: `@libsql/client` (async) crashes on parameterized FTS5 INSERT, the sync `libsql` npm package works, mitigation W15a (use sync package + run full FTS5 test suite) [SOURCE: research/003 - gaps-and-workarounds-sqlite-to-turso.md:634-653]. Treat as UNCHANGED until a web-enabled pass confirms.

**KNOWN / UNCHANGED (C4a, gap 6): `WITH RECURSIVE` is still hard-rejected at 0.7.0-pre.6.** COMPAT.md marks the WITH clause "🚧 Partial — WITH RECURSIVE not yet supported" [SOURCE: .opencode/specs/z_future/sqlite-to-turso/external/turso-main/COMPAT.md:131], and the engine fails at parse/plan time in two places with `bail_parse_error!("Recursive CTEs are not yet supported")` [SOURCE: external/turso-main/core/translate/planner.rs:751] [SOURCE: external/turso-main/core/translate/planner.rs:1364]. Gap 6 (HIGH severity) stands; the TypeScript-side graph-walk workaround in baseline doc 003 remains required.

INSTEAD OF` (view triggers) ❌ with a misleading "no such table" error [SOURCE: external/turso-main/COMPAT.md:135]; `changes()`/`total_changes()` are 🚧 "Still need to support update statements and triggers" [SOURCE: external/turso-main/COMPAT.md:269, 316]; RETURNING is rejected inside trigger bodies [SOURCE: external/turso-main/CHANGELOG.md:315]. keyFindings.text: **PARTIAL / CHANGED (C4c, gap 14): window functions are more limited than the baseline claimed, but more actively developed.** COMPAT.md: only `row_number()` and aggregate `… OVER (…)` work; `rank`, `dense_rank`, `percent_rank`, `cume_dist`, `ntile`, `lag`, `lead`, `first_value`, `last_value`, `nth_value` all missing; custom frame specs (`ROWS`/`RANGE`/`GROUPS BETWEEN`, `EXCLUDE`) not supported; and `agg(…) FILTER (…) OVER (…)` **panics** [SOURCE: external/turso-main/COMPAT.md:132, 244-245]. The baseline's claim that "`ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW` works" [SOURCE: research/003:623] is contradicted by the vendored COMPAT.md (no custom frame specs at all). Active churn in changelog (window-to-subquery rewrite fixes, named-window tests) suggests ongoing build-out [SOURCE: external/turso-main/CHANGELOG.md:86, 277, 370, 479]. Impact for spec-kit stays LOW: no live SQL window functions — ranking is done in TypeScript [SOURCE: research/003:619].

keyFindings.text: **KNOWN/UNCHANGED — `wal_checkpoint(TRUNCATE)` recovery path survives for local files.** `PRAGMA wal_checkpoint(TRUNCATE)` is exercised directly in vendored tests [SOURCE: core/mvcc/mod.rs:321; core/mvcc/persistent_storage/logical_log.rs:4658; core/connection.rs:4587 (aux schema)], and checkpoint modes incl. Full exist [wal.rs:5842]. Spec-kit's `rollbackBadApply` checkpoint (context-report.md:19) ports as-is for local-file DBs; the context-report's "no WAL checkpoint concept" caveat applies only to remote-replica deployments.

**NEW — C5c: there is no `err.code === 'SQLITE_BUSY'` in the Turso JS surface; the retry predicate must be rewritten.** Core errors: `LimboError::Busy` → "Database is busy" [SOURCE: core/error.rs:66-67], `BusySnapshot` → "Database snapshot is stale. You must rollback and retry the whole transaction." [error.rs:70-71], `WriteWriteConflict` → "Write-write conflict" (MVCC write contention) [error.rs:84-85], `TableLocked` → "Runtime error: database table is locked" [error.rs:62-63]; a numeric `SQLITE_BUSY = 5` exists only as a core-internal C-compat constant [error.rs:281-285]. The JS binding maps `StepResult::Busy` to a **generic napi error with message "database is locked" and no code property** (`Error::new(Status::GenericFailure, …)`) [SOURCE: bindings/javascript/src/lib.rs:212,225-227]. `runDaemonStateMutation`'s fresh-DB-per-attempt/3-retry predicate must match on message substrings ("database is locked", "Database is busy", and under MVCC "Write-write conflict"/"snapshot is stale") or on a wrapper-normalized code — message-matching is fragile and should be centralized.

**NEW — CHANGED (Gap 7 premise): AUTOINCREMENT now works under MVCC via atomic sequences.** The vendored test file literally strikes the old claim: "AUTOINCREMENT is not supported in MVCC mode due to sqlite_sequence / AUTOINCREMENT is supported in MVCC mode via atomic sequences" [SOURCE: core/mvcc/database/tests.rs:10412-10414], with passing-by-design tests `test_autoincrement_works_in_mvcc` [tests.rs:10416], `test_autoincrement_insert_works_for_preexisting_table` (pre-MVCC tables migrate) [tests.rs:10438-10441], `test_concurrent_autoincrement_inserts` [tests.rs:10498-10501], and `sqlite_sequence` reconciliation after checkpoint [tests.rs:10536-10539]. Baseline 003 Gap 7 ("AUTOINCREMENT does not work under MVCC", research/003:399-414) and 001:215 are out of date. Residual caveat: a comment flags an edge where "MVCC AUTOINCREMENT [is] able to re-emit a rowid already in use" in a recovery scenario [SOURCE: core/connection.rs:3555].

**NEW — CHANGED (Gap 8 premise): no trigger-under-MVCC rejection exists in the engine anymore.** An exhaustive sweep of MVCC `bail`/unsupported errors finds rejections only for: indexes without rowid [core/mvcc/cursor.rs:1211], custom index modules [core/translate/index.rs:66, core/schema.rs:1751], REINDEX [index.rs:619], CDC [core/translate/pragma.rs:595], virtual tables [core/translate/schema.rs:1692], WITHOUT ROWID tables [core/vdbe/execute.rs:1237], and ClearBtree [execute.rs:11320] — triggers are absent from that list, and MVCC tests actively create and recover triggers [tests.rs:1384-1396, 9309]. Verdict vs baseline "triggers do not fire under BEGIN CONCURRENT" (003:448): CHANGED, likely lifted — though this is inferred from absence-of-rejection plus test coverage, not an executed firing test.

**NEW — cross-gap interaction: Tantivy FTS and MVCC are mutually exclusive.** "Custom index modules are not supported in MVCC mode" [SOURCE: core/translate/index.rs:66; core/schema.rs:1751]. Since Turso FTS is delivered as a custom `index_method` module (established iteration finding), the baseline's W8b plan ("Tantivy removes trigger maintenance", 003:457) cannot be combined with MVCC mode at this version.

**NEW — mitigations for the retry predicate exist natively:** the JS binding accepts a `busy_timeout`-style open option wired to `Connection::set_busy_timeout` [SOURCE: bindings/javascript/src/lib.rs:255-268,330-331; core/connection.rs:3923], and `PRAGMA busy_timeout` is implemented [core/pragma.rs:146] — short contention can be absorbed before errors surface. Counter-nuance: at least one WAL contention class is explicitly non-retryable: "Retrying with busy_timeout will NEVER HELP." [wal.rs:3176] — the new predicate should distinguish retryable Busy from fatal lock states rather than blind-retrying 3×.

**NEW — REFUTED (baseline architecture assumption): "Turso will never support multi-process."** v0.7.0-pre.6 ships experimental **multi-process WAL coordination via a `.tshm` sidecar** ("cross-process WAL readers and writers") [SOURCE: external/turso-main/README.md:61]. Mechanism: sidecar at `{db_path}-tshm` [core/storage/wal.rs:116-118], an mmap'd "authority" (`MappedSharedWalCoordination`) that per-connection coordination delegates to and which "serializes across processes" [wal.rs:1277-1293]; readers register with the authority [wal.rs:2125]; open mode `SharedWalCoordinationOpenMode::MultiProcess` [wal.rs:2263, core/lib.rs:298]. The old in-code claim "we will never support multi process… don't need WAL's index file" survives only as a stale comment [wal.rs:2662-2663].

**PARTIAL — C5a maturity verdict: MVCC remains explicitly experimental.** It is now a journal mode whose literal is `experimental_mvcc` (serialized "mvcc") [SOURCE: core/storage/journal_mode.rs:26], enabled in tests via `PRAGMA journal_mode = 'experimental_mvcc'` [tests.rs:10484]; the repo's own agent guide labels it "experimental multi-version concurrency (WIP)" [SOURCE: external/turso-main/CLAUDE.md, Guides section]. Hardening is very active: BEGIN IMMEDIATE/CONCURRENT interlock [CHANGELOG.md:155], shuttle tests [CHANGELOG.md:119], MVCC REPL [CHANGELOG.md:110], recovery-heavy Whopper profiles [CHANGELOG.md:16-17], plus a `PRAGMA mvcc_checkpoint_threshold` tuning surface [COMPAT.md:230]. Baseline 002:99's re-evaluation criterion "MVCC stable with trigger support" is therefore half-met: trigger support appears present, stability is still self-declared experimental.

**PARTIAL — C5b verdict: the daemon/lease single-writer model SURVIVES (still required).** Multiprocess WAL is opt-in (`DatabaseOpts.enable_multiprocess_wal` / `with_multiprocess_wal(true)`, default off) [SOURCE: core/lib.rs:222,279-280], rejected for in-memory paths, unsupported IO backends, and unsupported filesystems [lib.rs:802-812], fails fast on mixed legacy/multiprocess opens ("Database is already open with[out] experimental multiprocess WAL in another process") [lib.rs:865,898], and — decisively — is **not exposed in the JavaScript bindings at all** (zero hits in bindings/javascript). Also COMPAT.md:69: "We don't support mixed SQLite and Turso in multi-process scenarios." So Node consumers remain effectively single-process-per-DB: `lease.ts` heartbeat-lease single-writer and code-graph daemon ownership remain necessary post-migration (their *synchronous transaction* idiom is a separate gap-16 issue, not a concurrency-model one).

**NEW — CHANGED (gap 10, C6b):** The CDC pragma has been de-unstabled: canonical name is now `capture_data_changes_conn` (per-connection, returns `(mode, table, version)`, default `off`), with `unstable_capture_data_changes_conn` retained only as an alias whose "name may change" [SOURCE: ...external/turso-main/COMPAT.md:224-225]. The baseline's "the word 'unstable' is literally in the API name" [SOURCE: ...research/003 - gaps-and-workarounds-sqlite-to-turso.md:503] no longer holds.

Because compat preserves sync `transaction`/`pragma`, the adapter's primary job is no longer sync→async bridging but **fencing five divergence ports**: (1) extension/vec0 KNN SQL, (2) FTS5 SQL + maintenance commands, (3) WITH RECURSIVE → app-side BFS behind a traversal port, (4) snapshot/recovery mechanics (WAL triplet, wal_checkpoint(TRUNCATE), backup→VACUUM INTO), (5) busy/retry predicate (SQLITE_BUSY → Busy/BusySnapshot). Revised adapter estimate: **1,200–2,000 LOC, 1–2 weeks** for the spec-kit server slice (≈85% of weight); do NOT wrap all 127 files — hotspots + ports only, long tail keeps a raw handle alias.

keyFindings.text: **NEW — degradation analysis even under a hypothetical OFF/FULL-only engine: graceful, non-fatal, durability-up.** Because Turso's pragma parser coerces unrecognized synchronous values to `SyncMode::Full` rather than erroring, a `.pragma('synchronous = NORMAL')` call can never throw out of the init sequence — worst case is silent escalation to FULL (slower commits, higher durability), not a failed bootstrap. Combined with the previous finding, the spec-kit init sequence has no synchronous-related failure mode on Turso at v0.7.0-pre.6. [SOURCE: external/turso-main/core/translate/pragma.rs:656,667]

keyFindings.text: **NEW — the question's premise is doubly moot: `ensure_vector_shard_schema` never sets `synchronous` at all.** Its pragma sequence on the attached shard schema is exactly `journal_mode = WAL`, `cache_size = -8192`, `mmap_size = 33554432`, `temp_store = DEFAULT`, `wal_autocheckpoint = 256` — no synchronous pragma. `synchronous = NORMAL` is issued only once, on the main connection at open time, alongside `busy_timeout/foreign_keys/cache_size/mmap_size/wal_autocheckpoint/temp_store`. [SOURCE: .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/search/vector-index-store.ts:498-503, 1579-1586]

keyFindings.text: **NEW — verdict CHANGED/REFUTED (supersedes the iteration 1-6 engine-gap list): `PRAGMA synchronous = NORMAL` IS accepted and honored at v0.7.0-pre.6.** The pragma write handler parses `"0"/OFF → SyncMode::Off`, `"1"/NORMAL → SyncMode::Normal`, anything else → `SyncMode::Full` (comment: "SQLite defaults to NORMAL for invalid values, but we want to default to a higher durability level so deviating here"), then calls `connection.set_sync_mode(mode)`; the read handler returns the stored mode. `SyncMode {Off=0, Normal=1, Full=2}` is defined in core, and `get_sync_mode()` is consulted in the pager's commit/sync path, so the value is plumbed into I/O behavior, not merely stored. The previously established "synchronous=NORMAL unsupported" engine gap no longer holds at the vendored version. [SOURCE: external/turso-main/core/translate/pragma.rs:650-672, 1499-1506; core/lib.rs:335-339; core/storage/pager.rs:2981,2999]

This covers the actual call sites: `PRAGMA shard.journal_mode = WAL` [SOURCE: db-shard-migration.ts:245], `pragma('${ACTIVE_VECTOR_SCHEMA}.journal_mode = WAL')` inside `ensure_vector_shard_schema` [SOURCE: lib/search/vector-index-store.ts:498-499], and `<schema>.wal_checkpoint(TRUNCATE)` — the latter already try/catch best-effort at every site [vector-index-store.ts:1681,1697,1731-1732,1743-1744]. Vendored `translate_vacuum` emits `Insn::VacuumInto { schema_name, dest_path }` unconditionally (no experimental flag) [SOURCE: external/turso-main/core/translate/vacuum.rs:28-37], while bare VACUUM requires `--experimental-vacuum` [vacuum.rs:39-44], is incompatible with multiprocess WAL [vacuum.rs:45-49], and schema-qualified in-place VACUUM is hard-rejected ("only supported for the main database") [vacuum.rs:51-57]. keyFindings.text: **KNOWN / UNCHANGED** — Current DDL is `CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE active_vec.vec_memories USING vec0(embedding FLOAT[${profile.dim}])` created in the attached shard [SOURCE: .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/search/vector-index-store.ts:516-522] and mirrored in the staging shard writer [SOURCE: .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/embedders/reindex.ts:437-443], alongside a plain canonical blob table `vec_<dim>(id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, vec BLOB NOT NULL)` [vector-index-store.ts:509-514; reindex.ts:383-394]. Turso has no vector index — linear scan only, vectors as plain BLOBs [SOURCE: external/turso-main/docs/sql-reference/functions/vector.mdx:392-394 Note; :40-47].

**KNOWN / UNCHANGED** — Today: FTS5 external-content table `CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE memory_fts USING fts5(title, trigger_phrases, file_path, content_text, content='memory_index', content_rowid='id')` plus 3 sync triggers `memory_fts_insert` / `memory_fts_update` / `memory_fts_delete` using the FTS5 `'delete'` command protocol [SOURCE: vector-index-schema.ts:1255-1281, migration v14]. Gates: `isMemoryFtsAvailable` probes `sqlite_master WHERE type='table' AND name='memory_fts'` [bm25-index.ts:478-480] and `shouldUseSqliteLexicalEngine` routes engines, throwing in `sqlite` mode without the table and falling back to in-memory JS BM25 in `auto` mode [bm25-index.ts:520-538, 508-509]. Weighted query: `SELECT m.*, -bm25(memory_fts, 10.0, 5.0, 2.0, 1.0) AS fts_score FROM memory_fts JOIN memory_index m ON m.id = memory_fts.rowid WHERE memory_fts MATCH ?` [SOURCE: sqlite-fts.ts:193-196; weights at bm25-index.ts:71-78].

**NEW (real gap)** — Staging-shard atomic-rename flow [reindex.ts:511-590]: batches write to `<active>.reindex-<jobId>.staging`, the shard is closed after `shard.pragma('wal_checkpoint(TRUNCATE)')` [reindex.ts:454], active `-wal`/`-shm` sidecars are unlinked, then `fs.renameSync(staging, active)` [reindex.ts:579-589]. The rename itself is filesystem-level and engine-agnostic, **but** the flow's correctness depends on the staging WAL being empty at close, and COMPAT.md marks `PRAGMA wal_checkpoint` as 🚧 Partial with "Not Needed calling with param (pragma-value)" — i.e. the parameterized `wal_checkpoint(TRUNCATE)` form is unsupported [SOURCE: external/turso-main/COMPAT.md:215]. Adjacent shard pragmas also break: `wal_autocheckpoint` ❌ [COMPAT.md:214; used at vector-index-store.ts:503] and `mmap_size` ❌ [COMPAT.md:183; used at :501]; `journal_mode`, `cache_size`, `temp_store` are ✅ [COMPAT.md:177,148,205]. Design fix: use the bare `PRAGMA wal_checkpoint;` (the supported form) and verify the returned frame counts, or rename the `-wal`/`-shm` sidecars together with the main file instead of relying on an empty WAL. This is a new P1 item for the Path C-prime adapter, on top of the established bare-VACUUM blocker at db-shard-migration.ts:274/:282.

**NEW** — `index_method` from the JS SDK / embedded use: confirmed first-class in the SDK, not CLI-only. `connect(":memory:", { experimental: ["index_method"] })` is exercised in the bindings' own FTS test [SOURCE: external/turso-main/bindings/javascript/packages/native/promise.test.ts:246-252]; `'index_method'` is in the `ExperimentalFeature` union [bindings/javascript/packages/common/types.ts:1]; the NAPI layer maps it to `core_opts.with_index_method(true)` [bindings/javascript/src/lib.rs:280]; the Rust sync Builder also gained `experimental_index_method` [COMPAT/CHANGELOG.md:138]. **PARTIAL**: whether the better-sqlite3-style compat entrypoint (`./compat`) forwards the `experimental` option array is unverified in the vendored tree — verification item for the adapter pilot.

**NEW** — `writeVectorsToKnn` rewrite [reindex.ts:365-381]: today it does `DELETE FROM vec_memories WHERE rowid = ?` then `INSERT INTO vec_memories (rowid, embedding) VALUES (?, ?)` per row with `BigInt(row.id)` inside `db.transaction()`. On a plain table this collapses to a single `INSERT OR REPLACE INTO vec_memories (rowid, embedding) VALUES (?, ?)` (vec0 forbade OR REPLACE; plain tables don't). `transaction(fn)` exists in the compat surface (established iter 1–9). Verification item: BigInt parameter binding through the compat layer is untested. The whole `sqliteVec.load()` / `vecAvailable` branching in `writeVectorsToShard` [reindex.ts:424-447] and the temp-view INSTEAD-OF alias layer [vector-index-store.ts:551-612] become deletable.

**NEW** — Compensation plan: encode today's weights index-time; schedule `OPTIMIZE INDEX` after bulk ingest/reindex to stabilize IDF; keep fusion rank-based (RRF tolerates absolute-score drift — the code already documents that BM25 scores are unbounded and must be normalized/RRF'd before mixing [bm25-index.ts:24-27]); re-validate any absolute fts_score thresholds; and regression-compare top-k overlap/NDCG (not raw scores) using the existing benchmark fixtures under `mcp_server/benchmarks/`.

**NEW** — Exact replacement DDL: `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS active_vec.vec_memories (rowid INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, embedding BLOB NOT NULL)`. vec0's implicit rowid becomes an explicit `INTEGER PRIMARY KEY` (rowid alias), so all existing `rowid`-addressed reads/writes keep their shape. Raw `Float32Array` buffers from `to_embedding_buffer()` insert directly — the f32-blob layout is sqlite-vec-compatible (established iter 1–9) and `vector_distance_cos` accepts raw f32 BLOBs of matching dimension; no `vector32(json)` round-trip needed [vector.mdx:130-161 plus established].

**NEW** — Expected ranking drift, largest first: (a) **per-segment statistics under NoMergePolicy** — Tantivy computes BM25 over per-segment term stats, and with merging disabled, identical documents in different segments score differently and IDF never converges to corpus-global until `OPTIMIZE INDEX`; (b) **default boolean operator** — Tantivy QueryParser treats multi-term queries as OR [fts.md:252] while FTS5 MATCH defaults to implicit AND, changing the candidate set, not just its order — compensate by having `sanitizeFTS5Query`'s successor emit explicit `AND` between tokens; (c) minor score perturbation from Tantivy's quantized (byte) fieldnorms vs FTS5's exact column-length normalization, with the same k1=1.2/b=0.75 defaults on both sides.

**NEW** — KNN query rewrite shape: `SELECT rowid, vector_distance_cos(embedding, :queryVec) AS distance FROM active_vec.vec_memories ORDER BY distance ASC LIMIT :k` [SOURCE: vector.mdx:339-347 canonical example]. Distance is `1 − cosine_similarity`, so existing similarity conversion is `similarity = 1 − distance` (today sqlite-vec's cosine distance has the same convention; no score-semantics change).
**NEW** — Quantized two-stage variant (concrete SQL), per the established vector8 lever: store a companion `embedding_q8 BLOB` (1 byte/dim → coarse scan reads 384 B/row instead of 1,536 B/row), then:
**NEW** — Replacement DDL: Turso FTS is an index on the base table, not a separate content table:

**NEW** — Structural difference: FTS5 weights are query-time arguments (`bm25(memory_fts, 10,5,2,1)` chosen per call); Turso weights are **index-time** (`WITH (weights='...')`, fixed at CREATE INDEX; changing them = DROP + re-CREATE = full re-index) [fts.md:117-134], with query-time `field:term^boost` syntax as a per-query escape hatch [fts.md:258]. Today's weights are a build-time constant (`BM25_FTS5_WEIGHTS`, never varied per query) [bm25-index.ts:71-78], so encoding `title=10.0,trigger_phrases=5.0,file_path=2.0,content_text=1.0` at index time is a lossless mapping for current behavior.

**NEW** — What breaks if FTS is still experimental at adoption: (1) the flag is per-connection — any process opening the DB without it (scripts, doctor tools, other runtimes) hits the `USING fts` DDL in the schema as an unknown index method, the schema-poisoning failure mode; (2) established mutual exclusion with MVCC forecloses the concurrent-writer lane for the whole DB; (3) unconditional NoMergePolicy means segments accumulate per 1,000-doc commit, requiring a new scheduled `OPTIMIZE INDEX` maintenance job that FTS5 never needed [fts.md:421-444, 450-457]; (4) no read-your-writes inside a transaction [fts.md:456] — any save-pipeline step that inserts a memory and lexically searches it pre-COMMIT silently misses it (post-insert enrichment/dedup paths need an audit); (5) no cross-version on-disk stability promise — though a full rebuild from `memory_index` is already a supported, cheap flow at this corpus size. **De-risk lever unique to this codebase:** `engine='auto'` already degrades to the in-memory JS BM25 when `memory_fts` is absent [bm25-index.ts:508-509], so the safe adoption sequence is: migrate without Turso FTS (JS BM25 carries lexical search), enable the FTS index behind the flag later.

**PARTIAL / CHANGED** — Dimension discovery: the fallback that regexes `FLOAT\[(\d+)\]` out of `sqlite_master.sql` [vector-index-store.ts:258-273] dies with the vec0 DDL; the authoritative source is already `vec_metadata.embedding_dim` checked first [vector-index-store.ts:213-256], so the migration only needs to delete the regex branch, not redesign discovery.

**NEW — 027 closure, causal tombstones: verdict UNCHANGED (covered by COMPAT).** `causal_edge_tombstones` DDL is a plain rowid table with `INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT` and a `DEFAULT (datetime('now'))` expression default, plus three ordinary composite indexes (one with `DESC` column ordering); writes are plain parameterized `INSERT INTO` [SOURCE: .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/causal/sweep.ts:67-101,237-259]. Generation counter uses `COALESCE(MAX(...))+1` [sweep.ts:184]; index existence is verified via `sqlite_master` [sweep.ts:60]. No partial/expression indexes, no generated columns, no json functions, no STRICT, no UPSERT. Supporting COMPAT rows: `datetime()` ✅ [SOURCE: external/turso-main/COMPAT.md:390], `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (UPSERT)` ✅ and `ON CONFLICT clause` ✅ [COMPAT.md:108-109] (relevant because adjacent pre-cataloged causal-edges.ts uses `INSERT OR IGNORE` at lib/storage/causal-edges.ts:571), AUTOINCREMENT parity noted [COMPAT.md:134].

keyFindings.text: **NEW — MVCC/FTS exclusivity analysis: MVCC loss is acceptable for this codebase, but "permanently off the table" is overstated.** Spec-kit's FTS dependence is real and central (hybrid-search.ts `ftsSearch` delegates to FTS5 bm25() weighted scoring; the whole FTS channel must be reimplemented on Turso FTS) [SOURCE: context/iterations/iteration-010.md:15]. If the migration adopts Tantivy-based Turso FTS (`index_method` flag), MVCC is unavailable while the established mutual exclusivity holds. However, this costs the codebase nothing today: the architecture is per-daemon single-writer with lease arbitration (established, survives migration), so every flow that could theoretically have wanted `BEGIN CONCURRENT` — (a) background `memory_ingest_start` overlapping interactive `memory_save`, (b) parallel deep-loop seats writing to the same memory DB, (c) retention/tombstone sweeps overlapping saves — is already serialized at the application layer by design. MVCC would only become attractive if the lease model were abandoned for true multi-writer daemons, which no current plan proposes. Caveat: the exclusivity is an artifact of the experimental Tantivy integration (NoMergePolicy-era); treat "permanent" as "until upstream decouples them" — a watch-item for the next web-enabled sweep, not an architectural commitment.

keyFindings.text: **NEW — CONFIRMED (iteration-9 side); iteration-1 OVERTURNED — CLAIM-3 (synchronous=NORMAL):** The write handler explicitly accepts NORMAL: numeric `"0"`→Off, `"1"`→Normal, and keyword matching `b"OFF"|b"0"`→Off, `b"NORMAL"|b"1"`→Normal, with everything else coerced to Full — a deliberate deviation from SQLite noted in-source ("SQLite defaults to NORMAL for invalid values, but we want to default to a higher durability level") [SOURCE: core/translate/pragma.rs:650-672]. keyFindings.text: **CLAIM-2** — KNOWN, **CONFIRMED**. `core/index_method/mod.rs` registers exactly three index-method modules: `backing_btree`, `fts`, `toy_vector_sparse_ivf` [SOURCE: external/turso-main/core/index_method/mod.rs:14-17]. The only vector index method documents itself as "Simple inverted index for sparse vectors", created via `USING toy_vector_sparse_ivf`, handling **jaccard** similarity over sparse-encoded vectors only [SOURCE: external/turso-main/core/index_method/toy_vector_sparse_ivf.rs:29-36]. Dense cosine search has no index path — `vector_distance_cos` is a plain scalar function [SOURCE: external/turso-main/core/vector/mod.rs:122-133], so `ORDER BY vector_distance_cos(...) LIMIT k` is a full linear scan at 0.7.0-pre.6.

keyFindings.text: **CLAIM-3** — KNOWN, **CONFIRMED**. `vector_serialize` emits Float32Dense as `Value::from_blob(x.bin_eject())` with **no header and no trailing type byte** — unlike Float64Dense (trailing `2`), Float32Sparse (dims + trailing `9`), Float1Bit (trailing `3`), Float8 (trailing `4`) [SOURCE: external/turso-main/core/vector/operations/serialize.rs:6-51]. The f32 bin data is a raw little-endian concatenation: `floats.iter().flat_map(|f| f.to_le_bytes()).collect()` [SOURCE: external/turso-main/core/vector/vector_types.rs:520], with a `bin_len == dims * 4` invariant [SOURCE: external/turso-main/core/vector/vector_types.rs:639]. That is byte-identical to sqlite-vec float32 blobs. Cosine: Float32Dense routes to simsimd `f32::cosine` (cosine *distance*) [SOURCE: external/turso-main/core/vector/operations/distance_cos.rs:23-26,85-87]; the in-repo Float8 path makes the formula explicit, `1.0 - dot / (norm1 * norm2).sqrt()` [SOURCE: external/turso-main/core/vector/operations/distance_cos.rs:77], and a test asserts opposite vectors yield distance 2.0 with the comment "Normal cosine distance [0, 2]" [SOURCE: external/turso-main/core/vector/vector_types.rs:698-723]. So `vector_distance_cos = 1 − cosine_similarity`, matching sqlite-vec's `vec_distance_cosine` ascending-order semantics.

keyFindings.text: **CLAIM-5** — KNOWN, **NUANCED** (weights + NoMergePolicy confirmed; per-segment-stats mechanism not directly read). Weights are declared in the CREATE INDEX `WITH` clause — doc comment `WITH (tokenizer='default',weights='col1=1.0,col2=2.0')` [SOURCE: external/turso-main/core/index_method/fts.rs:1335], parsed at attachment creation from `cfg.parameters.get("weights")` via `parse_field_weights` [SOURCE: external/turso-main/core/index_method/fts.rs:1377-1386,1226-1281] and stored as fixed `field_weights` applied as per-field boosts [SOURCE: external/turso-main/core/index_method/fts.rs:1483,1816] — i.e., fixed at index definition, not per-query like FTS5 `bm25(fts, w1, w2)`. `NoMergePolicy` is confirmed: imported [SOURCE: external/turso-main/core/index_method/fts.rs:31] and set on the writer, `writer.set_merge_policy(Box::new(NoMergePolicy))` [SOURCE: external/turso-main/core/index_method/fts.rs:2956], so segments never merge. The final link — BM25 statistics being computed per-segment and therefore drifting as unmerged segments accumulate — follows from the tantivy-style segment architecture but I did not read the scorer code itself this pass; treat that sub-clause as well-supported inference, not re-verified fact.

keyFindings.text: NEW — **Fallback evaluation and recommendation: choose (a).** (a) Keep ATTACH, enable `experimental: ["attach"]` at connect — effort is near-zero (one option in the connect call; add a startup capability probe), preserves all SQL shapes including cross-schema KNN joins and `VACUUM active_vec INTO`; residual risk is experimental-tier stability. (b) Separate connections per shard — must rewrite the hot KNN and multi-concept queries into two-phase app-level joins (shard KNN -> id/distance list -> hydrate from main), plus rework embedding-reconcile, checkpoints introspection, and the trigger matcher; medium-high effort and loses single-connection visibility between main and shard. (c) Consolidate the shard into main — medium effort, but an architectural regression against the explicit canonical-slimming design. Recommend (a), with (b) as the contingency if Turso's experimental attach proves unstable for write-bearing maintenance paths.
